title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Olivia Grant, who manages an animal shelter.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm talking to Olivia Grant, who manages an animal shelter. Olivia, did you always know you wanted to work with animals? || Guest: I loved animals as a child, but I first trained as a receptionist. [20]I started volunteering at weekends, and seeing frightened dogs become confident again made me want to work there full time[/20]. || Interviewer: What is the first thing you do when a new animal arrives? || Guest: We check its health, of course, but [21]the first important step is giving it a quiet space where it can feel safe[/21]. Some animals need time before people can handle them. || Interviewer: What do people misunderstand about shelters? || Guest: Many think we just play with animals all day. In fact, [22]a lot of the work is cleaning, keeping records and talking carefully to people who want to adopt[/22]. It is rewarding but not easy. || Interviewer: How do you choose the right home for an animal? || Guest: We ask lots of questions, and sometimes people are surprised. [23]We need to know about their daily routine, because a pet has to fit into real life, not just a weekend idea[/23]. || Interviewer: What part of the job makes you happiest? || Guest: Adoption days are lovely, but [24]my favourite moment is getting an update from a family months later, saying the animal has settled in well[/24]. That makes the hard days worthwhile. || Interviewer: What does the shelter need most at the moment? || Guest: Food donations are always useful, and blankets too. But [25]we especially need volunteers who can come regularly, even for just two hours a week[/25]. Animals do better with routine. || Interviewer: How do you cope when an adoption does not work out? || Guest: It is disappointing, but we tell people to be honest. If a dog returns, we learn more about what it needs. Sometimes the second home is perfect because we understand the animal better. Blaming people doesn't help anyone. || Interviewer: What advice would you give families thinking about getting a pet? || Guest: Take time. Visit more than once, ask questions and think about ordinary days, not just exciting first weeks. A pet needs care when you're tired, busy or on holiday. If people understand that, adoption can be wonderful. || Interviewer: Do children visit the shelter? || Guest: Yes, sometimes school groups come. We do not let them touch every animal, because that would be stressful, but we teach them how to behave around pets. Learning to be gentle is important. || Interviewer: What has changed since you started? || Guest: People now ask more questions before adopting, which is good. They want to know about training, costs and holidays. It means they are thinking seriously, and serious planning usually leads to happier animals and owners. || Interviewer: Is social media useful for the shelter? || Guest: Very useful, but we have to be honest. A cute photograph helps people notice an animal, but the description must include its real needs. Otherwise the wrong people apply, and that wastes time for everyone involved. || Interviewer: Do you have a favourite kind of animal to work with? || Guest: I like shy cats because progress is slow but very rewarding. When one finally comes to the front of its pen and asks for attention, it feels like a real success. Those small moments keep everyone motivated.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm talking to Olivia Grant, who manages an animal shelter. Olivia, did you always know you wanted to work with animals?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved animals as a child, but I first trained as a receptionist. I started volunteering at weekends, and seeing frightened dogs become confident again made me want to work there full time.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is the first thing you do when a new animal arrives?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We check its health, of course, but the first important step is giving it a quiet space where it can feel safe. Some animals need time before people can handle them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do people misunderstand about shelters?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many think we just play with animals all day. In fact, a lot of the work is cleaning, keeping records and talking carefully to people who want to adopt. It is rewarding but not easy.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you choose the right home for an animal?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We ask lots of questions, and sometimes people are surprised. We need to know about their daily routine, because a pet has to fit into real life, not just a weekend idea.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What part of the job makes you happiest?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Adoption days are lovely, but my favourite moment is getting an update from a family months later, saying the animal has settled in well. That makes the hard days worthwhile.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What does the shelter need most at the moment?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Food donations are always useful, and blankets too. But we especially need volunteers who can come regularly, even for just two hours a week. Animals do better with routine.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you cope when an adoption does not work out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It is disappointing, but we tell people to be honest. If a dog returns, we learn more about what it needs. Sometimes the second home is perfect because we understand the animal better. Blaming people doesn't help anyone.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give families thinking about getting a pet?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Take time. Visit more than once, ask questions and think about ordinary days, not just exciting first weeks. A pet needs care when you're tired, busy or on holiday. If people understand that, adoption can be wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do children visit the shelter?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, sometimes school groups come. We do not let them touch every animal, because that would be stressful, but we teach them how to behave around pets. Learning to be gentle is important.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What has changed since you started?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People now ask more questions before adopting, which is good. They want to know about training, costs and holidays. It means they are thinking seriously, and serious planning usually leads to happier animals and owners.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is social media useful for the shelter?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Very useful, but we have to be honest. A cute photograph helps people notice an animal, but the description must include its real needs. Otherwise the wrong people apply, and that wastes time for everyone involved.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you have a favourite kind of animal to work with?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I like shy cats because progress is slow but very rewarding. When one finally comes to the front of its pen and asks for attention, it feels like a real success. Those small moments keep everyone motivated.

questions.question: What made Olivia want to work at the shelter full time?

options.B: She had always wanted to be a vet.
options.C: She saw animals become happier and more confident.

questions.explanation: Olivia says seeing frightened dogs become confident again made her want to work there full time.

questions.question: What does Olivia say is important when animals first arrive?

options.A: They need somewhere quiet and safe.
options.C: They must be taken for a walk quickly.

questions.explanation: She says the first important step is giving a new animal a quiet space where it can feel safe.

questions.question: What do many people not realise about shelter work?

options.B: It includes many practical and administrative tasks.

questions.explanation: She says much of the work is cleaning, keeping records and talking to adopters.

questions.question: Why does the shelter ask adopters about their routine?

options.A: To check if they can pay for food.
options.B: To decide which volunteer should visit them.
options.C: To make sure the pet suits their everyday life.

questions.explanation: Olivia says a pet has to fit into real life, not just a weekend idea.

questions.question: What does Olivia enjoy most?

options.A: Hearing that adopted animals are doing well.
options.B: Watching families choose animals on adoption days.

questions.explanation: Her favourite moment is getting an update that an animal has settled in well.

questions.question: What does the shelter particularly need now?
questions.explanation: She says they especially need volunteers who can come regularly.
